meta.title: History Detectives
meta.subtitle: Learning About the Past from Clues • Printable Web Lesson

hero.ariaLabel: Vintage books and papers representing clues historians study
hero.imageQuery: old books photographs artifacts primary sources history hero

heroCopy.lede: History is the study of people, places, events, and choices from the past. Historians look for clues and use evidence carefully.
heroCopy.whatStudentsLearn: Students learn how historians use timelines, artifacts, photographs, maps, letters, and other primary sources to ask questions and build explanations about the past.
learningTargets: I can explain what history studies.
I can place events in order on a timeline.
I can identify artifacts and primary sources.
I can compare daily life long ago with daily life today.
studentDirections: Read the section and study the image before answering.
Use the vocabulary and quick facts to support your thinking.
Write in complete sentences unless the prompt says bullets are okay.
On paper: circle, label, or underline important evidence in the print image.

keyVocabulary.def: The study of people, places, events, and choices from the past.
A line that shows events in order from earliest to latest.
An object from the past that gives clues.
A source made by someone who saw or experienced an event.

sections.imageQuery: old books vintage papers history detective clues elementary
sections.title: What Is History?
sections.lede: History tells stories about the past, but historians do more than remember. They ask questions and look for evidence.

cards.title: Quick Facts
items: History includes world history, American history, and local history.
Historians use evidence instead of guessing.
The past can be studied from many points of view.

cards.title: Historian Tools
items: Questions help guide research.
Sources provide clues.
Evidence supports explanations.

prompt.label: Prompt A — Past or Present
prompt.placeholder: Name one thing from the past and one thing from today. How can you tell?
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Students distinguish past and present using evidence.

sections.imageQuery: pocket watch old photographs timeline sequence history
sections.title: Timelines Put Events in Order
sections.lede: A timeline shows events from earliest to latest. It helps readers see sequence, change, and cause and effect.

cards.title: Quick Facts
items: Timelines move in time order.
Dates, years, or time words can label events.
Timelines help us compare before and after.

cards.title: Sequence Mission
items: Find the first event.
Find the last event.
Use words like before, after, first, next, and finally.

sections.callout: A timeline can show world events, American events, community events, or your own life events.

prompt.label: Prompt B — Order Events
prompt.placeholder: Write three events in order from first to last.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Timelines use time words and sequence to show change.

sections.ariaLabel: Pocket watch and old papers representing timeline order and historical sequence

sections.imageQuery: historical artifacts old documents primary source museum books
sections.title: Artifacts and Primary Sources
sections.lede: Objects, letters, photos, maps, and recordings can help us learn what life was like. Each source gives clues and raises questions.

cards.title: Vocabulary
items: Artifact: an object people made or used.
Primary source: a source from the time being studied.
Evidence: clues that support an explanation.

cards.title: Look Closely
items: What do you notice first?
Who might have used this source?
What question would you ask next?

prompt.label: Prompt C — Source Clues
prompt.placeholder: Choose one source and explain what it might teach a historian.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: A source gives clues, but historians must observe carefully and ask questions.

sections.imageQuery: historic classroom wooden desks daily life long ago school history
sections.title: Daily Life Long Ago
sections.lede: Children around the world and in early America lived, worked, played, and learned in different ways. Some things changed, and some needs stayed the same.

cards.title: Compare
items: People needed food, shelter, community, and learning.
Homes, tools, transportation, and schools changed over time.
Different communities had different experiences.

cards.title: Think Like a Historian
items: Compare respectfully.
Use specific details.
Avoid assuming everyone lived the same way.

prompt.label: Prompt D — Compare Daily Life
prompt.placeholder: Compare one part of daily life long ago with daily life today.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Student compares with respect and gives one specific example.

sections.ariaLabel: Historic classroom with wooden desks showing daily life long ago

sections.imageQuery: old family photographs notebooks timeline personal history
sections.title: Quick Check + Family Timeline
sections.lede: We can study world history, American history, community history, and personal history by using sources and sequence.

cards.title: Mini Quiz
items: What is a timeline?
Name one kind of primary source.
Why should historians use evidence?

cards.title: Challenge
items: Create a three-event timeline.
Use clear labels.
Put events in order from first to last.

promptE.label: Prompt E — Summary
promptE.placeholder: Summarize how historians use timelines and sources.
promptE.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Summary includes past/present, timelines, sources, and evidence.

promptF.label: Prompt F — Create
promptF.placeholder: Create a short timeline with three events from your life, family, school, or community.
promptF.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Timeline has three ordered events with clear labels.

sections.ariaLabel: Old photographs and notebooks for creating a family timeline
